title: Monitor Artifacts storage consumption - Azure Artifacts
display_title: Monitor and manage Azure Artifacts storage limits
description: Learn how to monitor Azure Artifacts storage usage at Organization and Project levels.
summary:
Azure DevOps Services Azure Artifacts uses a consumption‑based billing model and supports storing various package types, including NuGet, npm, Python, Maven, Cargo, and Universal Packages. The free tier provides 2 GiB of storage but if you exceed this limit, you can either upgrade to a paid subscription or delete existing artifacts.
The artifact storage UI in your organization and project settings lets you monitor storage usage at both the organization and project levels. Storage is also grouped
classification_reason: Page describes Azure Artifacts’ consumption-based storage with a specific free-tier limit (2 GiB) and what happens when that limit is exceeded, which is product- and tier-specific quota information not derivable from general knowledge.

title: Publish Universal Packages in Azure Artifacts - Azure Artifacts
display_title: Publish Universal Packages and understand size limits
description: Learn how to publish Universal Packages to an Azure Artifacts feed.
summary:
Azure DevOps Services Universal Packages enable developers to store a wide range of package types beyond traditional formats like NuGet, npm, Python packages, etc.
Using Azure CLI, you can publish Universal Packages directly from the command line. These packages can be as large as 4 TiB, but they must always include a name and version number. This article guides you through publishing Universal Packages to your Azure Artifacts feed.
classification_reason: States explicit package size limit (4 TiB) and requirements (name and version), which are concrete product limits.

classification_reason: Documents the .artifactignore file syntax and behavior; includes pattern rules, precedence, and product-specific behavior for artifact publishing.

This server allows you to connect a debugger that can automatically fetch the correct symbol files, enabling you to debug your application efficiently. With tools like WinDbg, you can load an executable, attach the debugger to a running process, consume your symbols, set up breakpoints, and systematically analyze your code.
